request.question: What groundbreaking composer is the actor who plays Schwerner in Murder in Mississippi, best known for portraying?

title: James Rado
Murder in Mississippi
Roy K. Moore
Richard Rubin (writer)
Tom Bosley
Brendan Fehr
James Young (mayor)
Donald Hinds
Dolores Claman
Tom Hulce
sentences: James Rado (born James Alexander Radomski; January 23, 1932) is an American actor, playwright, director, writer and composer, best known as the co-author, along with Gerome Ragni, of 1967's groundbreaking American tribal love-rock musical "Hair".
He and Ragni were nominated for the 1969 Tony Award for best musical, and they won for best musical at the Grammy Awards in 1969.

Murder in Mississippi is a 1990 television film which dramatized the last weeks of civil rights activists Michael "Mickey" Schwerner, Andrew Goodman and James Chaney, and the events leading up to their disappearance and subsequent murder during Freedom Summer in 1964.
It starred Tom Hulce as Schwerner, Jennifer Grey as his wife Rita, Blair Underwood as Chaney, and Josh Charles as Goodman.
Hulce received a nomination for Best Actor in a TV Miniseries at the 1990 Golden Globes.

Roy K. Moore (June 11, 1914 Hood River Oregon - October 12, 2008 Madison Wisconsin) was an American FBI agent and former Marine who was best known as the chief agent who investigated the disappearance of civil rights workers James Chaney, Michael Schwerner and Andrew Goodman.
The 1988 film "Mississippi Burning", starring Gene Hackman and Willem Dafoe was based on that case.
Because of the efforts of Moore and his agents, nineteen men were indicted and seven were convicted.
All served less than 6 years in prison.
Richard Rubin (born 1967 in New York City) is an American writer.
He has published essays, articles, and short stories in a number of newspapers and magazines.

He is perhaps best known as the author of "The Last of the Doughboys: The Forgotten Generation and Their Forgotten World War", a history of America and World War I based upon interviews he conducted with its last veterans, and "", a personal memoir about the year he spent living and working as a newspaper reporter in the rural Mississippi Delta.

He is also known for his many short pieces, including "The Ghosts of Emmett Till," an acclaimed article he published in The New York Times Magazine in 2005, in which he revisits interviews he conducted in 1995 with the two surviving defense attorneys and the two surviving jurors from the 1955 Sumner, Mississippi trial of Roy Bryant and J.W. Milam, white men who were ultimately acquitted of the murder of the black 14-year old Emmett Till, despite overwhelming evidence of their guilt.
Bryant and Milam later confessed to the murder in an interview with journalist William Bradford Huie for Look Magazine.
"The Ghosts of Emmett Till" was anthologized in "The Best American Crime Writing 2006".
In 2014, Rubin wrote a series of pieces for The New York Times, for which he visited various American World War I battlefields in France.

The series, titled "Over There," was published in four installments between August and December, 2014; the final installment, titled "In France, Vestiges of the Great War's Bloody End," which deals with the Meuse-Argonne, was for a time the most emailed article in the newspaper.
Thomas Edward Bosley (October 1, 1927 – October 19, 2010) was an American actor, voice artist, television personality, and entertainer.
Bosley is best known for portraying Howard Cunningham on the long-running ABC sitcom "Happy Days", and the title character on the NBC/ABC series "Father Dowling Mysteries".
He also was featured in a recurring role on "Murder, She Wrote".
He originated the title role of the Pulitzer Prize–winning Broadway musical "Fiorello!
", earning the 1960 Tony Award for Best Performance by a Featured Actor in a Musical.

Brendan Jacob Joel Fehr (born October 29, 1977) is a Canadian film and television actor, perhaps best known for portraying Michael Guerin in the WB television series Roswell, and for portraying Laboratory Tech in .
In 2008, Fehr won a Gemini Award for "Hottest Canadian Male TV Star".
Brendan currently plays Dr. Drew Alister on the NBC drama The Night Shift.
James A. Young (born 1956) is an American politician, who was elected mayor of Philadelphia, Mississippi in May 2009.

His election was especially noted as he is the first African-American mayor of the city, which was previously best known for the murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner by members of the Ku Klux Klan in 1964.
Donald Hinds (born in 1934) is a Jamaican-born writer, journalist, historian and teacher.

He is best known for his work on the "West Indian Gazette" and his fiction and non-fiction books portraying the West Indian community in Britain, particularly his 1966 work "Journey to an Illusion", which has been called a groundbreaking book that "captured the plight of Commonwealth immigrants and foresaw the multicultural London of today".
Dolores Claman (born 6 July 1927) is a Canadian composer and pianist.
She is best known for composing the 1968 theme song for CBC's Hockey Night In Canada show, known simply as "The Hockey Theme", a song often regarded as Canada's second national anthem.
She is also known for"A Place to Stand", the popular tune that accompanied the groundbreaking film of the same name at Montreal's Expo 67 Ontario pavilion.
Both songs were orchestrated by Jerry Toth who, along with his brother Rudy Toth and composer Richard Morris, all worked together at Quartet Productions from 1965-1970.
Claman and her writing partner and husband, lyricist Richard Morris, composed over 3000 commercial jingles in a 30-year period and won more than 40 awards internationally for their work.
In the 1950s, Claman composed music for ITV while living in Britain and also wrote songs for West End musical revues.
Thomas Edward "Tom" Hulce ( ; born December 6, 1953) is an American actor and theater producer.

As an actor, he is best known for his role as Larry "Pinto" Kroger in "Animal House" (1978), his Academy Award-nominated portrayal of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart in "Amadeus" (1984), and his role as Quasimodo in Disney's "The Hunchback of Notre Dame" (1996).
Additional acting awards included four Golden Globe nominations, an Emmy Award, and a Tony Award.
Hulce retired from acting in the mid-1990s to focus on stage directing and producing.
In 2007, he won a Tony Award as a lead producer of the Broadway musical "Spring Awakening".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What groundbreaking composer is the actor who plays Schwerner in Murder in Mississippi, best known for portraying?
hop1_docs: Murder in Mississippi | Murder in Mississippi is a 1990 television film which dramatized the last weeks of civil rights activists Michael "Mickey" Schwerner, Andrew Goodman and James Chaney, and the events leading up to their disappearance and subsequent murder during Freedom Summer in 1964.  It starred Tom Hulce as Schwerner, Jennifer Grey as his wife Rita, Blair Underwood as Chaney, and Josh Charles as Goodman.  Hulce received a nomination for Best Actor in a TV Miniseries at the 1990 Golden Globes.

Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner | The murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner, also known as the Freedom Summer murders, the Mississippi civil rights workers' murders or the Mississippi Burning murders, involved three activists that were abducted and murdered in Neshoba County, Mississippi in June 1964 during the Civil Rights Movement.  The victims were Andrew Goodman and Michael "Mickey" Schwerner from New York City, and James Chaney from Meridian, Mississippi.  All three were associated with the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) and its member organization the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE).  They had been working with the Freedom Summer campaign by attempting to register African Americans in Mississippi to vote.  This registration effort was a part of contesting over 70 years of laws and practices that supported a systematic policy of disenfranchisement of potential black voters by several southern states that began in 1890.

James Young (mayor) | James A. Young (born 1956) is an American politician, who was elected mayor of Philadelphia, Mississippi in May 2009.  His election was especially noted as he is the first African-American mayor of the city, which was previously best known for the murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner by members of the Ku Klux Klan in 1964.

Biloxi Blues | Biloxi Blues is a semi-autobiographical play by Neil Simon.  It portrays the conflict of Sergeant Merwin J. Toomey and Arnold Epstein, one of many privates enlisted in the military stationed in Biloxi, Mississippi, seen through the eyes of Eugene Jerome, one of the other soldiers.  This play is the second chapter in what is known as his "Eugene trilogy", following "Brighton Beach Memoirs" and preceding "Broadway Bound", and is the only one in which Eugene is not the central character.  The play won the Tony Award for Best Play, and Barry Miller won a Tony Award for Best Featured Actor in a Play for his performance as Arnold Epstein.

James Chaney | James Earl Chaney (May 30, 1943 – June 21, 1964), from Meridian, Mississippi, was one of three American civil rights workers who were murdered during Freedom Summer by members of the Ku Klux Klan near Philadelphia, Mississippi.  The others were Andrew Goodman and Michael Schwerner from New York City.

United States v. Price | United States v. Cecil Price, et al., also known as the Mississippi Burning trial or Mississippi Burning case, was a criminal trial where the United States charged a group of 18 men with conspiring in a Ku Klux Klan plot to murder three young civil rights workers (Michael Schwerner, James Chaney, and Andrew Goodman) in Philadelphia, Mississippi on June 21, 1964 during Freedom Summer.  The trial, conducted in Meridian, Mississippi with U.S. District Court Judge W. Harold Cox presiding, resulted in convictions of 7 of the 18 defendants.
Neshoba (film) | Neshoba: The Price of Freedom is a 2010 documentary film about events and attitudes in Neshoba County, Mississippi, 40 years after the 1964 murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner.
hop1_titles: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found: Passage [1] identifies the Schwerner actor: "Tom Hulce as Schwerner" (passage [1]: Murder in Mississippi). Passage [1] also gives the film title and year: "Murder in Mississippi is a 1990 television film" (passage [1]).
- Ambiguities: No alternative actor candidates for Schwerner appear in the provided passages. The actor's own article/page (e.g., "Tom Hulce") is not present among the passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact: Bridge entity = "Tom Hulce" (exact token from passages). Missing fact needed = the name of the groundbreaking composer that Tom Hulce is best known for portraying (the composer's name and the role/production that establishes that association).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Tom Hulce" page (check lede/infobox and "Career"/"Notable roles" or similar sections) to find which composer he is best known for portraying. Search keywords: Tom Hulce lede, "Tom Hulce notable roles", "Tom Hulce best known for portraying".
  2) If needed, open the film/page for the role listed on Hulce's page (e.g., the film or stage production page where he portrayed the composer); check its lede and cast sections for the composer's name. Example search keywords: "<Film/Play title> cast <Tom Hulce>".
  3) If multiple role candidates appear, fetch their individual pages (role/film/play) and check lede/infobox and reception/awards sections to confirm which portrayal is described as the one he is "best known for."
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
response.hop2_query: Tom Hulce best known for portraying composer
hop2_docs: Tom Hulce | Thomas Edward "Tom" Hulce ( ; born December 6, 1953) is an American actor and theater producer.  As an actor, he is best known for his role as Larry "Pinto" Kroger in "Animal House" (1978), his Academy Award-nominated portrayal of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart in "Amadeus" (1984), and his role as Quasimodo in Disney's "The Hunchback of Notre Dame" (1996).  Additional acting awards included four Golden Globe nominations, an Emmy Award, and a Tony Award.  Hulce retired from acting in the mid-1990s to focus on stage directing and producing.  In 2007, he won a Tony Award as a lead producer of the Broadway musical "Spring Awakening".

Murder in Mississippi | Murder in Mississippi is a 1990 television film which dramatized the last weeks of civil rights activists Michael "Mickey" Schwerner, Andrew Goodman and James Chaney, and the events leading up to their disappearance and subsequent murder during Freedom Summer in 1964.  It starred Tom Hulce as Schwerner, Jennifer Grey as his wife Rita, Blair Underwood as Chaney, and Josh Charles as Goodman.  Hulce received a nomination for Best Actor in a TV Miniseries at the 1990 Golden Globes.

HULC (gene) | In molecular biology, "H"ighly "U"p-regulated in "L"iver "C"ancer (non-protein coding), also known as HULC, is a long non-coding RNA.  It was first identified in hepatocellular carcinoma, and is also expressed in colorectal carcinomas that metastasise to the liver.  It may have a role in the post-transcriptional regulation of gene expression.  It downregulates the expression of several microRNAs, including miR-372.  Expression of HULC is upregulated by CREB, there is a CREB-binding site in the promoter of HULC.  miR-372 represses translation of the kinase PRKACB, so downregulation of miR-372 leads to increased levels of PRKACB.  PRKACB activates CREB by phosphorylation, therefore leading to increased expression of HULC.

Tom Lister | Tom Lister (born 21 June 1978 in Ingleton, North Yorkshire) is an English actor who is best known for his portrayal of protagonist villain Carl King in the ITV soap opera "Emmerdale" (2004–12).

The Hunchback of Notre Dame (soundtrack) | The Hunchback of Notre Dame: An Original Walt Disney Records Soundtrack is the soundtrack to the 1996 Disney animated feature film, "The Hunchback of Notre Dame".  It includes songs written by Alan Menken and Stephen Schwartz with vocals performed by Paul Kandel, David Ogden Stiers, Tony Jay, Tom Hulce, Heidi Mollenhauer, Jason Alexander, Mary Wickes, and Mary Stout, along with singles by All-4-One/Eternal, and the film's score composed by Alan Menken.
Those Lips, Those Eyes | Those Lips, Those Eyes is a 1980 romantic comedy film directed by Michael Pressman, starring Frank Langella, Glynnis O'Connor, and Tom Hulce.
Tom Coster | Tom Coster (born August 21, 1941) is an American keyboardist and composer.  He is perhaps best known for playing with Carlos Santana.
response.summary_2: He is best known for portraying Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart. Tom Hulce is an American actor whose Academy Award–nominated portrayal of Mozart was in Amadeus (1984).
